keyIdeas: A half-equation shows either the oxidation or the reduction step separately, with electrons explicitly shown on one side.

To write an oxidation half-equation: write the species oxidised on the left, the product on the right, balance O by adding H‚āāO, balance H by adding H‚Āļ, then balance charge by adding electrons to the right.
To write a reduction half-equation: write the species reduced on the left, the product on the right, balance O with H‚āāO, balance H with H‚Āļ, then add electrons to the left to balance charge.
To combine half-equations into the overall ionic equation: multiply each half-equation so that the number of electrons is equal, then add and cancel electrons.
In acidic solution, H‚Āļ and H‚āāO are used to balance; in the specific reactions studied (metals + acids, metal displacement) the same rules apply.
Common oxidising agents at AS level: Cl‚āā (‚Üí 2Cl‚ĀĽ), H‚Āļ (‚Üí H‚āā), Fe¬≥‚Āļ (‚Üí Fe¬≤‚Āļ), MnO‚āĄ‚ĀĽ/Cr‚āāO‚āá¬≤‚ĀĽ (in context). Common reducing agents: metals (Fe ‚Üí Fe¬≤‚Āļ), Cl‚ĀĽ, I‚ĀĽ.

mustKnow.definition: An equation showing only the oxidation or reduction process, with electrons explicitly on one side; O balanced by H‚āāO, H balanced by H‚Āļ.
An equation showing only the species that change in a reaction; spectator ions are cancelled.
The species that appears on the left of a reduction half-equation, gaining electrons.
The species that appears on the left of an oxidation half-equation, losing electrons.

markSchemePhrases: electrons on correct side of half-equation
electrons cancel / equal electrons in each half-equation before combining
spectator ions omitted from ionic equation
commandWords: 'Write a half-equation' ‚ÄĒ include electrons explicitly on the correct side; balance O with H‚āāO and H with H‚Āļ. 'Combine half-equations' ‚ÄĒ multiply to equalise electrons, then add and cancel. 'Identify' the oxidising/reducing agent from an equation ‚ÄĒ name the species and state the change in oxidation number.
examinerTips: A common mark is awarded specifically for 'electrons on the correct side' ‚ÄĒ always double-check: oxidation half-equation has e‚ĀĽ on the right; reduction has e‚ĀĽ on the left.
After combining, verify that the number of each atom and overall charge are the same on both sides ‚ÄĒ a simple check saves marks.
For metal + acid reactions (e.g. Mg + H‚āāSO‚āĄ), write the reduction of H‚Āļ to H‚āā separately from the oxidation of Mg to Mg¬≤‚Āļ.
Spectator ions (Na‚Āļ, Cl‚ĀĽ not involved in redox) must be excluded from the overall ionic equation.
If the question says 'write a balanced ionic equation', this is NOT just the full molecular equation ‚ÄĒ you must cancel spectator ions.
workedExample:
Write the half-equations for the reaction of iron with dilute sulfuric acid and combine them into an overall ionic equation.

Oxidation half-equation (Fe loses electrons):
Fe ‚Üí Fe¬≤‚Āļ + 2e‚ĀĽ

Reduction half-equation (H‚Āļ gains electrons):
2H‚Āļ + 2e‚ĀĽ ‚Üí H‚āā

Both half-equations involve 2 electrons ‚ÄĒ no scaling needed.

Overall ionic equation (add and cancel e‚ĀĽ):
Fe + 2H‚Āļ ‚Üí Fe¬≤‚Āļ + H‚āā

Check: left: 1Fe, 2H, charge = 2+; right: 1Fe¬≤‚Āļ, 2H, charge = 2+. Balanced.
commonTraps: Putting electrons on the wrong side ‚ÄĒ in an oxidation half-equation, electrons are always on the right.

Forgetting to multiply one half-equation to equalise electrons before adding (e.g. combining Fe ‚Üí Fe¬≤‚Āļ + 2e‚ĀĽ with Cu¬≤‚Āļ + 2e‚ĀĽ ‚Üí Cu is fine; but Fe ‚Üí Fe¬≥‚Āļ + 3e‚ĀĽ with Cu¬≤‚Āļ + 2e‚ĀĽ ‚Üí Cu requires a factor of 2 and 3 respectively).
Including spectator ions in the 'ionic equation' ‚ÄĒ if the question asks for an ionic equation, remove all spectator ions.
Failing to balance H with H‚Āļ or O with H‚āāO in more complex half-equations.
Writing 'Fe¬≤‚Āļ' for a reduction half-equation involving Fe ‚ÄĒ in the reduction step Fe¬≤‚Āļ or Fe¬≥‚Āļ appears on the left (it is being reduced).
